splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether actuaries may select assumptions after that date. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be determined by the statutory measurement date (end of prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current data even if it means assumptions are chosen post-year-end.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Trustees of IAM National Pension Fund v. M & K Employee Solutions

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the supervision period beyond what was specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year and raises questions about judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled while the supervisee is a fugitive, so the clock stops running during the period of absconding.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and the term continues to run regardless.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether the gatekeeping provision of § 2244(b)(1), which categorically bars claims previously presented in a prior habeas petition, applies to federal prisoners bringing successive § 2255 motions. If it applies, federal prisoners face a stricter bar than what § 2255(h) alone would impose.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar on previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, in addition to the requirements of § 2255(h).

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, and such prisoners need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates can establish Article III standing to bring pre-election challenges to state election rules. The split affects the ability to challenge voting procedures before an election takes place versus being forced to litigate after the fact.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates have Article III standing to challenge state election laws that affect voting procedures, including mail-in ballot deadlines, based on competitive injury.

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates do not have Article III standing to challenge state election laws pre-election because the alleged injury is too speculative or attenuated.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity trumps deference to agency interpretations (including Sentencing Commission commentary) in the criminal context. At least three circuits hold lenity prevails, while others have deferred to anti-defendant agency readings. The split has deepened after Loper Bright overruled Chevron deference.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations in criminal sentencing contexts, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference applies even in criminal sentencing contexts, allowing anti-defendant interpretations of ambiguous Guidelines provisions to prevail over lenity.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to be met for disqualification) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' in the safety-valve provision means defendants must possess all three listed criminal-history criteria to be disqualified from safety-valve relief, or whether possessing any single criterion is disqualifying. This affects mandatory minimum sentencing for drug offenses across the country.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' conjunctively.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or' or in a distributive sense.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' Application Note 1 to § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy as 'controlled substance offenses' for career-offender purposes, is a valid and authoritative interpretation of the guideline text.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary adding conspiracy and other inchoate offenses to the definition of 'controlled substance offense' under § 4B1.2 is entitled to deference, given that the guideline text itself does not mention inchoate offenses. This affects whether defendants with prior conspiracy convictions qualify as career offenders.

positions.label: Commentary is authoritative — inchoate offenses included
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of conspiracy and other inchoate offenses as 'controlled substance offenses' is an authoritative interpretation entitled to deference under Stinson, even after Kisor.

positions.label: Commentary exceeds guideline text — inchoate offenses excluded
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and the commentary impermissibly expands the definition beyond its plain meaning.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines even if the substance is not controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) requires that the substance underlying a state conviction also be federally scheduled. Some circuits read the definition broadly to include any state-law drug offense, while others require a federal-law match.

positions.label: No federal-law limitation required
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b) broadly defines 'controlled substance offense' to include state-law offenses even if the specific substance is not controlled under federal law, as there is no textual basis to graft a federal-law limitation.

positions.label: Federal-law match required
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines only if the substance at issue is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to § 924(c)) may be considered as part of, or contribute to, 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether non-retroactive statutory changes in sentencing law can serve as or contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar reliance on such changes, while others allow them to be considered as one factor. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to resolve this but has itself become a source of disagreement.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether alone or in combination with other factors, cannot constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered as one factor among others in evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court, in revoking supervised release and imposing a sentence, may consider the need to punish the defendant for the violation of supervised release conditions, or whether revocation sentences must be based solely on the breach of trust.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether sentencing courts may expressly impose revocation sentences to punish defendants for their underlying violations of supervised release conditions, or whether the purpose must be limited to addressing the breach of the court's trust. The Sixth Circuit allows punishment for the violation itself, placing it at the extreme of a circuit split.

positions.label: May punish for the violation
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly consider the need to punish the defendant for the conduct underlying the violation when imposing a revocation sentence.

positions.label: Revocation based on breach of trust, not punishment
positions.summary: Holds that the purpose of a revocation sentence is to sanction the defendant's breach of the court's trust, not to punish for the underlying criminal conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the bankruptcy estate or the debtor under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A). This significantly affects debtors who convert their cases and the recovery available to creditors.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion increases in property value inure to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7, based on the broad scope of § 541(a).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the debtor under § 348(f)(1)(A), consistent with the statute's structure and policy of encouraging good-faith conversions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal deadline for filing a notice of appeal applies to an order resolving a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees, though arising in a criminal case, is a civil matter for purposes of the appellate filing deadline. The majority view treats it as a civil matter subject to the 30-day civil deadline, while at least one circuit applies the shorter 14-day criminal deadline.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, and the 30-day civil deadline for filing a notice of appeal applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that the 14-day criminal appeal deadline applies because the Hyde Amendment motion arises within and is part of the criminal case.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a federal prisoner challenging the conditions or execution of confinement (as opposed to the fact or duration of imprisonment) must proceed under habeas corpus or may bring a civil rights action (e.g., Bivens or § 1983).
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for federal prisoners challenging decisions affecting the conditions or execution of their confinement (such as BOP decisions on prerelease custody under the First Step Act), or whether prisoners may also pursue civil rights claims. This affects access to federal court remedies for thousands of prisoners.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive for execution-of-sentence challenges
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to BOP decisions about the execution of a sentence, including prerelease custody eligibility under the First Step Act, must be brought under habeas corpus and not through civil rights actions.

positions.label: Civil rights actions also available for conditions challenges
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may challenge conditions of confinement or execution-of-sentence decisions through civil rights actions (Bivens or § 1983) and are not limited to habeas corpus.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Carmona v. US Bureau of Prisons

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether orders denying motions to seal medical records in litigation are 'effectively unreviewable' on appeal from final judgment, satisfying the collateral order doctrine. The D.C. Circuit has held such orders are immediately appealable, while other circuits have been more restrictive about collateral order review of sealing denials.

positions.label: Immediately appealable under collateral order doctrine
positions.summary: Holds that maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order' warranting immediate appellate review because such privacy cannot be restored once the information is disclosed.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal are not immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because alternative means exist to protect the information pending final judgment.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any . . . broker,' preempts common-law negligent hiring and selection claims against freight brokers when the broker's driver causes an accident. This affects the liability landscape for the freight brokerage industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because they relate to the broker's 'service' of arranging transportation and would effectively regulate broker operations.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that common-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted because they do not regulate prices, routes, or services within the meaning of the FAAAA, and traditional tort duties fall outside the statute's preemptive scope.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for a sentence reduction under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release motions may encompass claims that overlap with habeas corpus relief under § 2255, such as arguments that the underlying conviction or sentence was legally erroneous. Some circuits permit consideration of such factors as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' inquiry, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used as a substitute for habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider legal errors in the underlying conviction or sentence as one factor in the compassionate release analysis, as the statute imposes no categorical bar on such considerations.

positions.label: May not consider — use § 2255 instead
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for challenging the legality of the conviction or sentence itself, and such claims must be raised through habeas corpus under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal prohibition on firearm possession by unlawful users of controlled substances, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen, circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by unlawful drug users is constitutional as applied to marijuana users. Some circuits have upheld the statute, while others have found it unconstitutional, creating divergent rules on gun rights for marijuana users across the country.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because historical tradition supports disarming those who use intoxicating substances.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming those who use controlled substances.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FIFRA's labeling requirements preempt state tort claims alleging that a pesticide manufacturer should have included additional warnings on its label. This affects products liability litigation involving herbicides and pesticides nationwide, including high-profile Roundup litigation.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because allowing such claims would impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those required under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by FIFRA because they parallel, rather than impose requirements 'in addition to or different from,' federal labeling requirements.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, exempting them from mandatory arbitration.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to construe the FAA's Section 1 exemption for transportation workers. The question of whether last-mile delivery workers who distribute goods to retail stores are 'transportation workers' engaged in interstate commerce has divided the circuits, with implications for millions of workers in logistics and distribution chains.

positions.label: Broad exemption — last-mile distributors qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who distribute goods that have traveled in interstate commerce, including last-mile delivery workers, are 'transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce' exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow exemption — last-mile distributors do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that the Section 1 exemption is limited to workers in the transportation industry who are directly engaged in the movement of goods across borders or state lines, not to workers who merely handle goods that previously traveled in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of the right to make and enforce contracts?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standard for § 1981 claims in hospitality and service contexts, particularly regarding what constitutes interference with the right to make and enforce contracts and the available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach against other circuits' frameworks.

positions.label: Narrower standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 claims require a showing of interference with an actual contractual relationship or attempted contract, applying a more restrictive view of what constitutes actionable discrimination.

positions.label: Broader standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 covers a broader range of discriminatory conduct in business establishments, including degraded service or hostile treatment that impairs the enjoyment of contracted services.
